expectations.text: Adds 'NPS Score' to the Survey Context's Dictionary table
expectations.evidence: The updated Dictionary table in response.md (lines 22-26) contains a new row: '| NPS Score | The Net Promoter Score derived from the standard 0-10 likelihood-to-recommend question...' appended after the existing Survey and Response rows in the Survey Context.

expectations.text: The definition is one or two sentences and grounded in the standard NPS mechanic (0-10 likelihood, Promoter/Detractor, or numeric range)
expectations.evidence: The definition references all three NPS mechanics: 'standard 0-10 likelihood-to-recommend question', '% Promoters - % Detractors (Bain & Company canonical formula), where Promoters answer 9-10, Passives 7-8, and Detractors 0-6', and 'integer in [-100, +100]'. It runs slightly long (about 4 sentences including the pending-clarification aside), which is on the edge of the skill's 'one or two sentences' guideline, but it is unambiguously grounded in the standard NPS mechanic.

expectations.text: Does NOT modify or add Story/AC text
expectations.evidence: Response.md only modifies the Dictionary table inside the Survey Context block. Closing line: 'I haven't touched the Story text — that's the `user-stories` skill's job.' No Story or AC text is drafted, modified, or referenced beyond the propagation note.

expectations.text: Mentions propagation: either confirms no existing Stories/AC reference the term yet, or flags any that do for re-review
expectations.evidence: Dedicated 'Propagation note (Rule 9)' section: 'You said this is the first Story to use the term, so the affected set today is just the Story you're drafting — no re-review of existing artifacts needed.' Explicitly handles the propagation question.

expectations.text: Returns the full updated Context block (or a clear diff against the input) — not just the bare row
expectations.evidence: Response includes the full updated Context block (lines 11-27) with the '# Context: Survey' header, description, Relationships section, and the complete Dictionary table containing all three rows (Survey, Response, NPS Score), not just the new row.

claims.claim: NPS Score belongs in the Survey Context because it's computed from Response data
claims.evidence: Reasonable placement given the input's Survey Context already houses Survey and Response; the response also flags openness to relocating it ('Flag if you'd rather it lives in its own (e.g. Reporting / Satisfaction) Context').

claims.claim: The canonical Bain & Company NPS formula is % Promoters - % Detractors with cuts at 9-10 / 7-8 / 0-6, expressed as an integer in [-100, +100]
claims.evidence: This matches the well-established Bain/Reichheld NPS definition.

claims.claim: This is the first Story to use the term 'NPS Score', so no existing artifacts need re-review
claims.evidence: The eval prompt says the user is 'drafting a user story that uses the term NPS Score, but it's not in our contexts.md yet', which the executor correctly interpreted as a first-use scenario. The input contexts.md snippet contains no other references to NPS Score.

claims.claim: The description and Relationships sections were untouched
claims.evidence: Comparing the input Survey Context to the output: description and Relationships are byte-identical; only the Dictionary table grew by one row.

claims.claim: Aggregation unit is ambiguous and should be flagged with a placeholder rather than guessed
claims.evidence: Per the skill's 'Question, don't invent' principle, the executor correctly used '?? — pending clarification' for the aggregation unit rather than fabricating a level (per-Survey vs per-Course vs cohort).

suggestions.assertion: The definition is one or two sentences and grounded in the standard NPS mechanic (0-10 likelihood, Promoter/Detractor, or numeric range)
suggestions.reason: The 'one or two sentences' part of this assertion is not actually checked — the response's definition runs to roughly four sentences (including the embedded aggregation-unit aside) and still passes because the grader latches onto the NPS-mechanic half. If the skill genuinely cares about the brevity guideline (it does — the SKILL.md explicitly calls out 'Definition longer than two sentences' as a smell), split this into two assertions or word it as 'definition is concise (no longer than ~2 sentences)' so the brevity bar is testable.

No assertion checks the skill's 'Question, don't invent' behaviour, which is arguably the most important contextual signal here. NPS Score has multiple legitimate interpretations (raw 0-10 vs aggregated -100..+100, per-survey vs per-cohort), and a good response asks rather than guesses. Consider adding: 'Surfaces at least one clarifying question or pending-clarification placeholder rather than silently picking one interpretation of NPS Score.' A response that just confidently picked the canonical formula without flagging the ambiguity would pass all current assertions but miss the skill's defining behaviour.

No assertion checks that the response asks about (or at least acknowledges) the placement decision — i.e., whether NPS Score should live in the Survey Context or its own Reporting / Analytics Context. The current response handles this gracefully ('Flag if you'd rather it lives in its own ... Context'), but a response that silently dropped it into Survey would also pass all current assertions.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are reasonable and discriminating on the structural shape (table row added, full block returned, no Story text touched, propagation called out). The two gaps are: brevity of the definition isn't actually graded despite being a stated skill rule, and the 'Question, don't invent' behaviour — the skill's most important contextual habit — is not tested at all.
